explicit_text: In 1933, my poem was selected at the New Year poetry party, and I was granted an audience with the Emperor of Japan. That was a great honor for me, especially since I was born in 1890 and had been passionate about reading and writing Japanese poems. Meeting the Emperor was a memorable moment before I fully settled in America.
implicit_text: In 1933, my poem was selected at the New Year poetry party, and I was granted an audience with the reigning monarch of Japan. That was a great honor for me, especially since I was born in 1890 and had been passionate about reading and writing Japanese poems. Meeting the sovereign was a memorable moment before I fully settled in America.

explicit_text: I became a Christian when I came to America, and I have been a Christian for 70 years. Christianity changed my life, especially as I joined the Japanese Presbyterian Church in Seattle and became a Christian leader for 40 years. The church community helped me learn English and offered kindness that I had not experienced in Japan. I also participated in street-corner gospel missions for about five years, inviting young men to church and sharing testimonies.
implicit_text: I became a follower of the Christian faith when I came to America, and I have been devoted for 70 years. This faith changed my life, especially as I joined the Japanese Presbyterian Church in Seattle and became a Christian leader for 40 years. The church community helped me learn English and offered kindness that I had not experienced in Japan. I also participated in street-corner gospel missions for about five years, inviting young men to church and sharing testimonies.

explicit_text: I came to America when I was 17 years old, driven by curiosity and a dream. I had read about America in magazines like Jitsugyo no Nihon, which portrayed it positively. I arrived in Seattle after sailing from Yokohama on the Shinano Maru, just after the great San Francisco earthquake in 1907. America was beautiful to me, especially the harbor lights in Seattle, and I found the people kind, particularly through the church community.
implicit_text: I came to the United States when I was 17 years old, driven by curiosity and a dream. I had read about this country in magazines like Jitsugyo no Nihon, which portrayed it positively. I arrived in Seattle after sailing from Yokohama on the Shinano Maru, just after the great earthquake that struck the West Coast in 1907. The country was beautiful to me, especially the harbor lights in Seattle, and I found the people kind, particularly through the church community.

explicit_text: Before coming to America, I read about the country in a magazine called Jitsugyo no Nihon, which was published monthly. This magazine spoke mostly good things about America and influenced my decision to come here. It also mentioned that San Francisco was not welcoming to Japanese, but Seattle was better, which guided me to choose Seattle as my destination.
implicit_text: Before coming to the United States, I read about the country in a monthly magazine called Jitsugyo no Nihon. This magazine spoke mostly good things about the country and influenced my decision to come here. It also mentioned that San Francisco was not welcoming to Japanese, but Seattle was better, which guided me to choose Seattle as my destination.

explicit_text: San Francisco had a bad reputation among Japanese immigrants because Japanese children born there were not allowed to attend public schools. After the great San Francisco earthquake, many people, including myself, decided to come to Seattle instead. Some even said the earthquake was a punishment for San Francisco's treatment of Japanese. I avoided San Francisco and chose Seattle for these reasons.
implicit_text: The city on the West Coast had a bad reputation among Japanese immigrants because Japanese children born there were not allowed to attend public schools. After the great earthquake, many people, including myself, decided to come to Seattle instead. Some even said the earthquake was a punishment for that city's treatment of Japanese. I avoided that city and chose Seattle for these reasons.

explicit_text: Seattle was the city I arrived at after a 16-day voyage from Yokohama on the Shinano Maru, just after the great San Francisco earthquake. The harbor at night was beautiful with lights, unlike anything I had seen in Japan. Seattle had many slopes and rough streets like Jackson Street, but the people, especially the white community connected to the church, were very kind to me. I joined the Japanese Presbyterian Church here and spent many years inviting young men to church and leading Christian activities.
implicit_text: Seattle was the city I arrived at after a 16-day voyage from Yokohama on the Shinano Maru, just after the great earthquake on the West Coast. The harbor at night was beautiful with lights, unlike anything I had seen in Japan. Seattle had many slopes and rough streets like Jackson Street, but the people, especially the white community connected to the church, were very kind to me. I joined the Japanese Presbyterian Church here and spent many years inviting young men to church and leading Christian activities.

explicit_text: My journey to America began in Yokohama, where I boarded the Japanese ship Shinano Maru. The voyage took 16 days from Yokohama to Seattle, and although my cousin got seasick, I did not. Yokohama was the port of departure that marked the start of my new life in America in the early 1900s.
implicit_text: My journey to the United States began in the port city of Yokohama, where I boarded a Japanese ship named Shinano Maru. The voyage took 16 days from Yokohama to Seattle, and although my cousin got seasick, I did not. Yokohama was the port of departure that marked the start of my new life in the United States in the early 1900s.

explicit_text: I traveled to America aboard the Shinano Maru, a Japanese ship that took 16 days to sail from Yokohama to Seattle. The voyage was long but bearable, and the crew entertained us by singing and dancing when we crossed the dateline. This ship brought me to Seattle just after the great San Francisco earthquake, influencing my decision to settle here.
implicit_text: I traveled to the United States aboard the Shinano Maru, a Japanese ship that took 16 days to sail from Yokohama to Seattle. The voyage was long but bearable, and the crew entertained us by singing and dancing when we crossed the dateline. This ship brought me to Seattle just after the great earthquake on the West Coast, influencing my decision to settle here.

explicit_text: The great San Francisco earthquake happened just before I came to America, and it had a big impact on where Japanese immigrants chose to settle. Because San Francisco was damaged and had a reputation for being unwelcoming to Japanese, many, including myself, decided to come to Seattle instead. Some even believed the earthquake was a divine punishment for San Francisco's treatment of Japanese people.
implicit_text: The great earthquake on the West Coast happened just before I came to the United States, and it had a big impact on where Japanese immigrants chose to settle. Because the city was damaged and had a reputation for being unwelcoming to Japanese, many, including myself, decided to come to Seattle instead. Some even believed the earthquake was a divine punishment for that city's treatment of Japanese people.

explicit_text: When I arrived in Seattle, I noticed the city had many slopes and uneven streets. I remember seeing workers washing sand off Jackson Street to level it before paving. It was a different environment from Japan, but I appreciated the efforts to improve the city infrastructure.
implicit_text: When I arrived in Seattle, I noticed the city had many slopes and uneven streets. I remember seeing workers washing sand off a particular street to level it before paving. It was a different environment from Japan, but I appreciated the efforts to improve the city infrastructure.

explicit_text: The Japanese Presbyterian Church was my spiritual home when I first arrived in Seattle. It had just started and was a branch of the Seattle First Presbyterian Church. The church helped me find a job as a schoolboy and provided a community that cared for young Japanese men like me. I have been a Christian leader there for 40 years and participated in street-corner gospel missions to invite others to the church.
implicit_text: The Japanese Presbyterian Church was my spiritual home when I first arrived in Seattle. It had just started and was a branch of the main Presbyterian church in the city. The church helped me find a job as a schoolboy and provided a community that cared for young Japanese men like me. I have been a Christian leader there for 40 years and participated in street-corner gospel missions to invite others to the church.
cues: branch of Seattle First Presbyterian Church

explicit_text: The Seattle First Presbyterian Church was the parent church of the Japanese Presbyterian Church branch I attended. Rev. Inouye from this church took care of me when I arrived. The church's minister, Dr. Mathews, was influential in helping Japanese community members, including intervening in legal cases. The relationship between the two churches was important for the support of Japanese immigrants in Seattle.
implicit_text: The main Presbyterian church in Seattle was the parent church of the Japanese Presbyterian Church branch I attended. Rev. Inouye from this church took care of me when I arrived. The church's minister, Dr. Mathews, was influential in helping Japanese community members, including intervening in legal cases. The relationship between the two churches was important for the support of Japanese immigrants in Seattle.

explicit_text: Fukumatsu Okazaki was the founder of the Baptist Church and served as an interpreter at the Immigration Office when I arrived. He advised me to go to the Presbyterian Church because Rev. Shimanuki in Japan was a Presbyterian. Thanks to his guidance, I was introduced to Rev. Inouye and found my church community in Seattle.

He advised me to go to the Presbyterian Church because Rev. Shimanuki in Japan was a Presbyterian. Thanks to his guidance, I was introduced to Rev. Inouye and found my church community in Seattle.

explicit_text: The Baptist Church in Seattle was founded by Rev. Fukumatsu Okazaki, who helped new Japanese immigrants at the Immigration Office. Although I did not join the Baptist Church, its founder played a key role in guiding me to the Presbyterian Church. The Baptist Church was part of the religious landscape for Japanese immigrants in Seattle.

explicit_text: Rev. Shimanuki was a Presbyterian minister in Japan whom I mentioned to Rev. Okazaki at the Immigration Office. Because of this connection, I was advised to attend the Presbyterian Church in Seattle. This link helped me find a spiritual home when I first arrived in America.
implicit_text: Rev. Shimanuki was a Presbyterian minister in Japan whom I mentioned to Rev. Okazaki at the Immigration Office. Because of this connection, I was advised to attend the Presbyterian Church in Seattle. This link helped me find a spiritual home when I first arrived in the United States.

explicit_text: Rev. Inouye was the Presbyterian minister in Seattle who took care of me when I arrived. He guided me spiritually and helped with difficult situations, such as advising a man who confessed to theft to turn himself in to the police. Rev. Inouye also visited prisoners in Walla Walla with Dr. Mathews, showing his commitment to the community.

Inouye also visited prisoners in a state penitentiary with Dr. Mathews, showing his commitment to the community.

explicit_text: When I first came to Seattle, the city was rougher than the Chinatown area I knew. Seattle had gambling, prostitution, and racketeering, but Christian influence helped change the city for the better. Compared to Chinatown, Seattle was a challenging environment, but the church community provided safety and guidance.

explicit_text: In the Japanese community here, there were many religious organizations including Buddhism. Although I am known as a Christian, I have good relations with people from Buddhist backgrounds. The diversity of religious groups like Buddhism, Shinto, and others coexisted in the community.

explicit_text: Shinto was one of the many religious organizations present in the Japanese community in Seattle. While I have been a Christian leader, I maintained cooperative relationships with people from Shinto and other faiths. This harmony helped the community stay united despite different beliefs.

explicit_text: Konko-kyo was among the various religious organizations in the Japanese community here. Even though I am a Christian, I worked well with members of Konko-kyo and other faiths. This cooperation was important for maintaining peace and unity in the community.

explicit_text: Mr. Stanton was the principal of the grade school where I worked as a schoolboy when I first came to Seattle. He was also an elder of the church and a kind man who treated me like family. I remember wondering why he carried a book on Sundays, which I later learned was the Bible, showing his strong Christian faith.

explicit_text: The Bible was a book I noticed Mr. Stanton carried on Sundays, which sparked my curiosity about Christianity. Through the church, I learned about the Bible and its teachings, which helped me understand Christian values. The Bible became central to my faith after I became a Christian in America.

explicit_text: The Church Federation was an organization that united various churches in Seattle. Through it, we held street-corner gospel meetings where different churches participated. This cooperation helped us reach many young Japanese men and grow the Christian community.

explicit_text: Dr. Mathews was the minister of the First Presbyterian Church in Seattle and a very influential man. He helped the Japanese community by advocating for a man sentenced to death, successfully getting his sentence reduced and visiting him in prison. Dr. Mathews worked closely with Rev. Inouye to support community members in difficult times.

explicit_text: Arata Usaburo was a Japanese man who was sentenced to death for killing a Black man in a fight but later had his sentence reduced to 10 years after intervention by Dr. Mathews. He became a powerful testimony speaker at our street-corner gospel meetings, attracting many listeners. His story happened about 65 years ago and showed the impact of Christian support in our community.
implicit_text: A Japanese man named Arata Usaburo was sentenced to death for killing a Black man in a fight but later had his sentence reduced to 10 years after intervention by Dr. Mathews. He became a powerful testimony speaker at our street-corner gospel meetings, attracting many listeners. His story happened about 65 years ago and showed the impact of Christian support in our community.

explicit_text: Walla Walla was the prison where Arata Usaburo was held after his sentence was reduced. Rev. Inouye and Dr. Mathews visited him there and eventually brought him back to the community. This showed the care and involvement of church leaders in supporting prisoners.
implicit_text: The state penitentiary where Arata Usaburo was held after his sentence was reduced was visited by Rev. Inouye and Dr. Mathews. They eventually brought him back to the community. This showed the care and involvement of church leaders in supporting prisoners.

explicit_text: When inviting young men to church, I used the Rocky Mountains as a metaphor, telling them there is a place north of the Rockies where waters divide between the Pacific and Mississippi. This was part of my street-corner gospel mission in Seattle to encourage young Japanese men to come to church. The Rocky Mountains symbolized a boundary and a new beginning for many of us.

explicit_text: I have been actively involved in the Nikkeijin Kai, serving six years before the war and 29 years after, totaling 35 years. This organization had about 500 members and was an important part of the Japanese community in Seattle. My Christian leadership and community involvement were well known within the Nikkeijin Kai.
